query: This Jira ticket just says 'users should be able to see their stuff offline' — my PM wants me to make it buildable before sprint planning. Can you pin down exactly what we're shipping?
Can you write up the As a / I want / So that narrative plus the Given/When/Then scenarios for our image feed feature?
We keep arguing about what 'load the feed' actually means. I need a use case with the happy path and every error course nailed down — offline, expired token, empty response.
Before we build this, define the data contract: the GET endpoint, status codes, and the JSON the backend returns, including which fields are optional.
turn this lousy one-liner requirement into something my team can actually estimate and test
Draw me a flowchart of the decision flow for the checkout feature including the failure and retry branches, and a model spec table for the Order entity.
The stakeholders described the comments feature in business terms. I need acceptance criteria with concrete user types so QA has something to test against.
what questions should I be asking to make this half-baked feature request unambiguous before any code gets written?
Spec out the password-reset feature end to end so it's implementable — who the users are, the steps, the contract, and how it can fail.
Our product brief for 'favorites' is too open to interpretation. Help me make it precise and testable.
Wire up the composition root for this app and inject the dependencies into the feed loader.
Write the SwiftUI view for the feed screen with a list, loading state, and pull-to-refresh.
Migrate these XCTest cases to Swift Testing with #expect and #require.
Where should the networking code live and how do I split this monolith into Swift packages with protocol boundaries?
My view controller is 800 lines and untestable — how do I break it up and add test doubles?
Implement the URLSession HTTP client and decode this JSON into my Feed model.
Fix this Sendable warning at my actor boundary and make the mapper @MainActor.
MVVM vs MVP for this screen — which should I actually use and why?
Add accessibility labels and Dynamic Type support to my SwiftUI settings view.
Write a parameterized Swift Testing suite covering the date-formatter edge cases.
